ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for murtabak or paneer murtabak. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic flatbread food product class. Paneer murtabak is a pan-fried stuffed flatbread variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) is the closest defensible match. No specific murtabak or paneer-stuffed flatbread code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for murtabak or paneer murtabak. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this dish.

serving.description: 1 piece of murtabak with a small side of curry gravy
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of paneer murtabak is approximately 350 g, comprising one full piece of pan-fried stuffed flatbread accompanied by a small portion of curry gravy.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; HPB Singapore Food Composition Database; USDA FoodData Central — component-based estimation
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains a direct entry for paneer murtabak as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by component-based synthesis: wheat flour dough values from ICMR-NIN (chapati/paratha analogues), paneer values from ICMR-NIN (paneer, fresh), egg values from USDA FoodData Central (whole egg, cooked), and spice/oil contributions from USDA FoodData Central. The input record values (energy 214.3 kcal, protein 8.6 g, fat 12.9 g, carbohydrate 17.1 g per 100 g) are consistent with this component-based estimate and are retained. Confidence is medium due to estimation basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — chapati / paratha analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer murtabak. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the closest analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021: chapati (GI ~52–62) and paratha (GI ~62), which share the same wheat flour dough base and pan-frying preparation. The paneer and egg filling contributes protein and fat that moderates glucose response. A midpoint GI of 62 is used as a conservative medium-GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 60.0 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 37.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.9 mg/100 g; saturated fat 5.71 g/100 g; low dietary fibre 1.14 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a piece (~175 g) to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per eating occasion, request reduced salt in the dough and filling, avoid or minimise the curry gravy accompaniment which adds further sodium, and substitute ghee with a small amount of unsaturated cooking oil to reduce saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour dough with medium GI (~62); carbohydrate 17.1 g/100 g; low dietary fibre 1.14 g/100 g; glycemic load 37.3 per standard 350 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to half a piece (~175 g) to reduce glycemic load to approximately 18–19 per eating occasion, request whole wheat flour dough to increase fibre and lower GI, avoid high-GI curry gravy accompaniment, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to further moderate postprandial glucose response; the paneer and egg filling provides protein that partially moderates glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.71 g/100 g (exceeds AHA 5 g/100 g threshold); trans fat 0.14 g/100 g; cholesterol 71.43 mg/100 g; sodium 342.9 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume paneer murtabak sparingly and infrequently; request substitution of ghee with a small amount of canola or olive oil to reduce saturated and trans fat, limit portion to half a piece to keep saturated fat intake below 10 g per eating occasion, and avoid the curry gravy accompaniment to minimise additional sodium and saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.71 g/100 g marginally exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; refined carbohydrate dough contributes to inflammatory glycaemic load; AGE formation from pan-frying with ghee
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute ghee with extra-virgin olive oil to reduce saturated fat and AGE formation, retain anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, cumin, curry leaves) in the preparation, avoid chilli if nightshade sensitivity is present, and limit portion size to reduce overall inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Murtabak is a Malaysian-Singaporean pan-fried stuffed flatbread made by stretching thin wheat flour dough, filling it with a savoury mixture of paneer (fresh Indian cheese), beaten eggs, diced onion, and aromatic spices including cumin, turmeric, chilli, and coriander, then folding the dough into a square or rectangular parcel and pan-frying in ghee or cooking oil until golden brown and crispy on the outside. It is typically served with a side of curry gravy for dipping. The dish is a vegetarian adaptation of the classic murtabak, which traditionally uses minced mutton or chicken; the paneer filling provides a mild, milky, slightly firm texture that contrasts with the crispy, layered dough exterior. Nutritionally, paneer murtabak provides approximately 214 kcal per 100 g, with 8.6 g protein from paneer and eggs, 12.9 g fat (5.7 g saturated, primarily from paneer and ghee), and 17.1 g carbohydrate from the wheat flour dough. Dietary fibre is low at 1.14 g/100 g and sodium is moderately elevated at 343 mg/100 g. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~62) due to the refined wheat flour base, and a high glycemic load per standard 350 g serving (~37), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension (sodium and saturated fat), type 2 diabetes (medium GI, low fibre, high GL per serving), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and trans fat from ghee). It is halal-certified and vegetarian-suitable, making it a popular choice across Muslim and Hindu communities in Singapore and Malaysia. Common serving contexts include breakfast, supper, and hawker centre meals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in one piece of murtabak?
Is paneer murtabak high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains a direct entry for paneer murtabak as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-based synthesis from ICMR-NIN (paneer, chapati/paratha analogues), USDA FoodData Central (eggs, spices), and HPB Singapore database. The input record values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is estimated from analogous foods (chapati/paratha) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for paneer murtabak as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish are not reported in the approved sources consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the approved sources consulted as of 2026. The original input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved source and supports the paneer and spice component values; it is retained as a contributing source. The input data_source field listed 'grabfood' which is a forbidden source; all nutrient values have been re-anchored to approved sources via component-based estimation. Ethnic tags in the original input ('Noodle', 'Fried Rice', 'Roti Prata') were incorrect for this dish and have been removed. The dish is classified as Malaysian-Singaporean cuisine with country of origin Singapore/Malaysia. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (62 × 60.0 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 37.3; available carbohydrate in 350 g serving = 17.14 × 3.5 = 59.99 g ≈ 60.0 g.
